feedback.text: Imagine this; one day you decide to go back and listen to your old Vinyl or CD collection. However, now the record deck or CD player can phone home via the Internet to Universal Music (or whichever record label) to check that you're still allowed to listen to that music. Then you discover that, without any warning, they have already thrown the kill switch and disabled your old Vinyl or CDs. You would then have a situation where you had paid good money to own something, but the purchase has been taken away from you without your consent. That would be outrageous, would it not? Fortunately, what I described above is not technically possible for old media like Vinyl or CDs. However, this is exactly what is happening for video games. The player device for a video game is more complicated than a record deck or CD player its a full-blown computer which can be made to take hidden actions, by the game software, like sending messages over the Internet to a server controlled by the game publisher in order to check you have a legal copy of the game for example. Doing this as an anti-piracy measure is fair enough but this also gives the game publisher access to a remote kill switch where they can, without warning, shut down that server and prevent the game you paid for from ever working again. This has been an increasing problem since the turn of the millennium. Just like books and music, video games contain culture. I am from generation X and grew up in the 80s and early 90s and for the video games of my childhood I can share these with my own children because they were all made during a time when the Internet was not commonplace. I think that millennials and generation Z will not be able pass on their culture this way because many video games from 2000 onwards will no longer work, yes you can install them on an old computer but when you try to play - the game will try to phone home which then fails because the publisher long since shut down the authentication server. The net result is a loss of culture belonging to these generations which I think is a tragic shame. The success of the stop killing games campaign shows that legislation to oblige game publishers to end this practise has popular public support. I also think this issue is the canary in the coalmine for a much wider issue of products taking hidden actions against the best interest of their owners, there is planned obsolescence practises in everything from laptop computers to tractors.
